name: Velikovsky Worlds
description: Velikovsky Worlds, stemming from the work of Russian-American psychiatrist Immanuel Velikovsky, posits that Earth experienced catastrophic near-collisions with other celestial bodies (primarily Venus and Mars) in historical times. These events, he argued, caused widespread geological upheavals, cultural trauma, and inspired many ancient myths and religious texts. Velikovsky's theories, first presented in his 1950 book *Worlds in Collision*, challenge conventional uniformitarian geology and astrophysics. He interpreted myths and legends across various cultures (including the Old Testament) as literal accounts of these cosmic catastrophes. Velikovsky proposed that Venus was ejected from Jupiter as a comet and subsequently interacted with Earth and Mars before settling into its current orbit. Later, Mars allegedly caused further disturbances. Proponents claim historical records, such as Egyptian papyri and biblical accounts, support these events. Mainstream science overwhelmingly rejects Velikovsky's theories. Astronomers point out that his proposed orbital mechanics violate established laws of physics. Geologists find no evidence of global catastrophes of the scale he describes during the proposed timeframes. Historians and archaeologists argue that Velikovsky selectively interpreted texts, ignoring contradictory evidence and imposing his pre-determined conclusions onto them. His work is largely considered pseudoscience due to its reliance on unsubstantiated claims and disregard for scientific methodology.
subtitle: Catastrophic cosmic encounters in recent history

claims.claim: Venus originated as a comet ejected from Jupiter.
claims.evidence: Velikovsky cites similarities between descriptions of Venus in ancient texts and cometary appearances.
claims.criticism: This contradicts established models of planetary formation and orbital mechanics. No known mechanism could eject a planet-sized object from Jupiter in the manner Velikovsky describes.

claims.claim: Earth experienced near-collisions with Venus and Mars, causing global catastrophes.
claims.evidence: Velikovsky interprets mythological accounts of floods, earthquakes, and darkness as records of these events. He also points to geological anomalies he attributes to sudden, global upheavals.
claims.criticism: Geological evidence does not support global catastrophes of the magnitude and frequency Velikovsky proposes within the given timeframe. Mythological interpretations are subjective and often based on selective readings of texts.

claims.claim: These cosmic events influenced ancient cultures and religions.
claims.evidence: Velikovsky draws parallels between mythological narratives and his proposed cosmic events, claiming they are memories of real occurrences.
claims.criticism: Correlation does not equal causation. Similarities in myths can arise from shared human experiences or cultural diffusion, not necessarily from literal historical events. Velikovsky's interpretations often ignore the symbolic and allegorical nature of mythology.

keyFigures.name: Immanuel Velikovsky
keyFigures.role: Author of *Worlds in Collision* and primary proponent of Velikovsky Worlds.

keyFigures.name: Carl Sagan
keyFigures.role: Astrophysicist and prominent critic of Velikovsky's theories.

mythologyConnections: Egyptian mythology (particularly accounts of chaos and upheaval)
Biblical accounts of the Exodus and the Flood
Greek mythology (stories of planetary gods and cosmic battles)

timeline.event: Height of controversy and debate surrounding Velikovsky's theories
Velikovskian ideas persist in fringe and alternative communities

sources.author: Henry H. Bauer
sources.work: Beyond Velikovsky: The History of a Rejected Paradigm
sources.title: Henry H. Bauer
